title: British Pancakes

overview.description: Thin, lightly crisp-edged British pancakes made from a simple batter of flour, milk and eggs.

ingredients: Butter or neutral oil, for cooking
method: Place the flour and salt in a large mixing bowl.
Make a well in the centre.
Whisk gradually, drawing in the flour from the sides, until you have a smooth, thick batter.
Gradually whisk in the remaining milk until the batter is smooth and fairly thin.
Leave the batter to rest for at least 20 minutes if possible.
Heat a non-stick frying pan over medium-high heat.
Add a small amount of butter or oil.
Pour in enough batter to coat the base of the pan when tilted and swirled.
Cook for approximately 30–60 seconds until the edges begin to crisp and the underside is golden.
Cook the other side for approximately 20–30 seconds.

learning.did-you-know: British pancakes are closer to French crêpes than to thick American-style pancakes. Traditionally, they are particularly associated with Shrove Tuesday.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to make a thin batter and control pan temperature to produce thin pancakes with lightly crisp edges.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A simple family recipe that is quick, inexpensive and versatile enough for both sweet and savoury fillings.
chapman-tips: Resting the batter helps the flour fully hydrate and can make the pancakes more tender.
The first pancake is often a test pancake while you adjust the heat.
The batter should be thin enough to spread quickly across the pan.
Use only a small amount of butter or oil between pancakes.
If the batter thickens while standing, add a small splash of milk.
For lemon and sugar pancakes, add the lemon juice and sugar while the pancake is still hot.
The pancakes can be stacked and reheated later.
The same batter can be used for savoury pancakes by changing the filling.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well. Stack with baking paper between the pancakes and freeze in an airtight container.
notes.leftovers: Reheat in a frying pan or microwave. Leftover pancakes can be used for both sweet and savoury fillings.
